verses.text: We went up to Bashan: Og king of Bashan came out at Edrei. The LORD: fear him not, I will deliver him — do unto him as Sihon. We took sixty cities, the kingdom of Og, fenced with high walls, gates, and bars. Guidance: A new giant, same God. Og was a legend — and a legend fell. Sixty fortified cities did not save the king whose bed was iron. Fear not is not a feeling to summon; it is the conclusion God has already drawn from what He has already done.

We took the land of two Amorite kings on this side Jordan, from Arnon to Hermon. Only Og remained of the remnant of giants; his bedstead was iron, nine cubits long, four cubits wide. Guidance: A nine-cubit bed — about thirteen feet. Israel inherits not just land but the proof of who used to own it. Giants are real, and God still wins. The iron bed in Rabbah outlasts the king who slept in it.

This land we possessed. To Reuben and Gad: from Aroer by Arnon, half of Gilead. The rest of Gilead, all Bashan kingdom of Og — to the half tribe of Manasseh; Bashan called the land of giants. Jair took all the region of Argob, called it Bashan-havoth-jair to this day. Guidance: God's gifts move from giant-territory to family-territory overnight. What used to be 'land of giants' becomes 'inheritance of Reuben.' Names change with owners. The settled life begins by writing your name where the giant's bed used to be.

I commanded you: the LORD your God hath given you this land to possess. Ye shall pass over armed before your brethren, all that are meet for war. Your wives, little ones, cattle shall abide in your cities I have given you, until the LORD hath given rest unto your brethren also; then return every man to his possession. Guidance: The 2.5 tribes get their land first — but they don't move in first. Swords forward, families behind. The earliest-settled fight the longest. God's family takes care of its slow members; the strong wait at the river until the rest cross.

I commanded Joshua at that time: thine eyes have seen all the LORD your God hath done unto these two kings; so shall He do unto all the kingdoms whither thou passest. Ye shall not fear them, for the LORD your God shall fight for you. Guidance: Moses tells Joshua: look at what He did. Memory is the seed of courage. The next campaign will not be a leap into the unknown — it will be a continuation of the pattern. The LORD He shall fight for you — Joshua will hear this sentence a thousand times across his coming chapters.

I commanded Joshua at that time: thine eyes have seen all the LORD your God hath done unto these two kings; so shall He do unto all the kingdoms whither thou passest. Ye shall not fear them, for the LORD your God shall fight for you. Guidance: Moses tells Joshua: look at what He did. Memory is the seed of courage. The next campaign will not be a leap into the unknown — it will be a continuation of the pattern. The LORD He shall fight for you — Joshua will hear this sentence a thousand times across his coming chapters.

I besought the LORD: let me go over, see the good land beyond Jordan. But the LORD was wroth with me for your sakes: let it suffice thee, speak no more of this matter. Get thee up to Pisgah, lift up thine eyes — for thou shalt not go over. Charge Joshua, encourage him. So we abode in the valley over against Beth-peor. Guidance: Moses prays for a no he already heard once, and gets it again. 'Speak no more of this' is one of the tenderest hard lines in the Torah. God does not change His mind — but He gives Moses the mountaintop view. Sometimes the answer to our biggest prayer is the highest view we ever get.
